g.data-narration: Linear Filtering is a fundamental image processing technique where each output pixel is a weighted sum of its input neighbors.
In convolution, a small kernel or filter slides over the image. At each position, the output pixel equals the weighted sum of the neighborhood pixels under the kernel.

Common linear filters include the Box filter which averages all neighbors equally, the Gaussian filter which gives more weight to the center pixel, and the Sharpening filter which emphasizes the center and subtracts neighbors.
The convolution formula computes output g at position x y as the double summation of image f at shifted positions multiplied by kernel h, iterating over all kernel indices i and j.
g.text: summing over all kernel indices i, j

g.data-narration: At image borders the kernel extends beyond the image boundary. Padding strategies handle this: zero padding fills exterior with zeros, replicate copies the nearest border pixel, reflect mirrors pixel values, and wrap-around uses pixels from the opposite side.

Linear filtering powers many computer vision tasks: blurring and noise reduction via box or Gaussian filters, image sharpening to enhance details, and edge detection using gradient operators like Sobel and Prewitt.
